---
title: "Untitled"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Skills inventory

For each skill area, describe what your group actually used it for. If you did not use a skill, say so and note why.

### Functions & refactoring (W2)

*What we did:*

While the concepts learned in this lecture and practical about functions were really interesting we unfortunately couldn´t really use them in our project, because there were no codes we had to use twice or more often in the entire project. Every research question was quite different to the others and so were the analysis methods. Still we tried to implement the principles of DRY and DRRY.

*What worked well:*

Not repeating ourselves (DRY) worked quity well. For example we started with a very broad Dataset and only used a small part of it. The filtering and selecting process we did in a seperate document and we didn't have to repeat ourselves, since we uploaded the seperate document on github and linked it in the proposal.

*What was hard:*

Finding silent errors. Luckily we didn't come across many of these, but of course we still had to check for them in every single code chunk of the project while working on it and since you don't always know the expected outcome (for example when using {infer} for a correlation test in Research Question 2) you don't know whether it is correct or not.

### Debugging (W3)

*What we did:*

The debugging skills became very useful during our project, where we repeatedly had to identify problems in our scripts, fix plotting errors and resolve issues caused by package dependencies or changes in our code. It also reminded me that understanding an error message is often the first and most important step towards solving a problem.

*What worked well:*

Understanding and fixing Errors with the help of LLMs. Thankfully with the help of ChatGPT and Claude there were no errors that stopped us or slowed us down.

*What was hard:*

Because of working in different quarto documents (proposal, analysis_website, results_website etc.) with similar contents we often got the error message "object 'xy' not found", because for example we had the chunk where an object was defined in only one of those three quarto documents at first, even though we were using it in all three. Of course after noticing it that is an easy fix, but working across different documents was a little hard in the beginning.

### Version control & remotes (W4)

*What we did:*

Learning to work with git changed our whole workflow. Just pulling the most recent version of the project before working on it and pushing it afterwards makes collaboration fairly easy. One of us worked only within the command line with git and one of us mostly used github desktop. Both worked out fine.

*What worked well:*

We forced merge conflicts really early to understand how to handle them and didn't have any problems with those afterwards.

*What was hard:*

In the beginning it only seemed hard to understand the whole structure of git and how it is connected to github. After some weeks of working with it we got used to it.

### Quarto websites & collaborative coding (W5)

*What we did:* Our entire project is a Quarto website, so this was the week whose content we used most directly. We built separate pages for the proposal, the analysis, the results and the reflection, linked through a navigation bar defined in `_quarto.yml`, and published the site so it is viewable on the web.

*What worked well:* Rendering the website our website for the first time felt like an immediate success and wasn't hard at all. Once everything was setup and we had understood the structure of a quarto website it was also not complicated to change small things later on and for example add more files to the navigation bar of the site.

*What was hard:* Correctly adding all the right chunk options to all chunks correctly was a little bit annoying. Even though some of those are already set for the whole file in the `yaml`-header (for example `warning: false`). For example we first had to decide for every chunk, whether we want to show its code on the website or not and then put `#| echo: false` (or `true`).

### R packages (W6)

*What we did:* We did not use this skill in our project. We built a package in the practical, but our analysis is a one-off website rather than code anyone would install and reuse, so the packaging overhead would not have bought us anything.

### Initial data analysis & data cleaning (W7)

*What we did:* We really learned that this part is essential for any Data Analysis project, as you can't just go and analyse data and try to answer research questions without first confirming the suitability and quality of the Data.

*What worked well:* After checking for `NA`s in the variable we used for answering Research Question 1, `people_fully_vaccinated_per_hundred`, we noticed missing data for all Dates before December 2020. This makes sense as the Covid vaccination campaign only started around this time. In other words this missing data was no problem for our Analysis, but it was important to check and find out.

*What was hard:* I wouldn't call any part of this process hard, but it took quite a long time and was quite a lot of work, more than expected.

### Reproducibility, open data & renv (W9)

*What we did:* We set up `renv` for the project, generated `renv.lock` and committed it so that anyone cloning the repository can restore the same package versions.

*What worked well:*

*What was hard:* Even though the idea behind it and the problems it solves are great, working with `renv` really wasn't for us. For a long time we had problems with it, `renv::status()` often warned us that something wasn't right and honestly we understood it so poorly in the beginning that I couldn't even write down what went wrong. Still we now have a `renv.lock` file in our Repo, so somehow we managed. This was made worse by one of us changing laptops during this period, so hardware problems and `renv` problems compounded each other.

### Analysis & inference strategies (W10)

*What we did:* This is where our actual analysis happened. For RQ2 we tested whether vaccination coverage is associated with mortality (RQ2a) and with ICU occupancy (RQ2b), using Pearson correlation as the test statistic and a permutation test built with `{infer}` to generate the null distribution. We report effect size, sample size, the p-value and the seed rather than the p-value alone. Both associations are negative and moderate to strong (r ≈ −0.53 for RQ2a, r ≈ −0.65 for RQ2b, n = 832 over the overlap window from late December 2020 to early April 2023).

*What worked well:* Since the analysis with `{infer}` is not easy we got a lot of help from LLMs to not have to think to much about the code itself which we were working with for the very first time, but to achieve an analysis, that really tries to answer Research Question 2

*What was hard:* Interpreting the results of this analysis.

### Communication & visualisation (W11)

*What we did:* We reworked the plots from our analysis into presentable figures: proper axis labels with units instead of raw variable names, titles that state what the figure shows, and `fig-alt` descriptions on every figure. We did not use `{gt}` for our project, as we decided to go with a plot rather then a table in every important analysis step, because it fittet better to communicate the results of our Research Questions (Development over time, correlation) .

*What worked well:* Improving the plots we had already made was a very smooth process without any error messages for once. Especially the `#| fig-alt` option was interesting and new to us.

## Key analytical decisions

Describe 2–3 decisions that had a meaningful effect on your results or workflow.

### Decision 1: using smoothed variables

*What were we deciding between?*

Whether we should use the smoothed variables, which are calculated by the mean within one week of the non smoothed ones with the same value then every day, or the non smoothed variables.

*How did we reason through it?*

We decided to use the smoothed variables, because, for example, our daily vaccination activity plot (@fig-rq1-activity in results) would have many jump points otherwise.

*In hindsight, would we decide differently?*

Not at all, because our decision made plots look better and smoother, and it had no influence on the interpretation, even if it can hide short term changes (which were not that important to us).

### Decision 2: \[short title\]

*What were we deciding between?*

*How did we reason through it?*

*In hindsight, would we decide differently?*

## Workflow & collaboration

*How did we divide work across group members?*

We divided the work between the two of us almost per task: one of us should implement the website, the other one RQ1 again the other one RQ2a and RQ2b. But often we did this with test files, which we reviewed together before pushing them to github. Like this we could work parallel almost the entire time, but the final decisions we always made together.

*What branching or Git workflow did we actually use — and did it match the proposal?*

One of us worked only within the command line with git and one of us mostly used github desktop. Our main workflow consisted of pulling the newest version, editing files locally, committing changes and pushing them to GitHub. Most development took place on the `main` branch, while the `gh-pages` branch was used to publish the Quarto website. Working only on the main branch was possible because we were a small group of two.

*What would we change about how we coordinated?*

One thing we would change, to be more clear who works on which file at which time. Because by not doing so at certain times, we got merge conflicts and were uncertain if one is working on the newest version. But to be honest, different from that we would not change much, because that, for example, some project structure changes by time rather made our project better than holding onto some structure from the very start (for example referring to making out of three RQ only two).

LLM usage

Describe up to 5 significant uses of an LLM tool during the project.

### Usage 1: debugging

-   **Tool / model:**

    ChatGPT and Claude

-   **What it produced:**

    We used an LLM to help identify and correct errors in our codes, for example when filtering data, or creating ggplot visualisations.

-   **What we had to correct or verify:**

    We had to test LLM written code, whether a plot looked like we imagined it to look, or if some outcome in general looked like we had imagined it.

### Usage 2: improving visualisations

-   **Tool / model:**

    ChatGPT and Claude

-   **What it produced:**

    We used an LLM to suggest improvements to plot labels, legends, axis formatting and themes. We then selected and modified the suggestions to make the figures consistent with the rest of the website.

-   **What we had to correct or verify:**

    We had to look at the plots ourself and had to verify that it looked the way we imagined it.

### Usage 3: solving problems with git, renv, ...

-   **Tool / model:**

    -   ChatGPT and Claude

-   **What it produced:**

    When we encountered and ran into same error with git, like our first merge conflict or not being able to push for some reason, the LLM could always solve the problem quickly. The same it was with renv where we were always in the wrong path and did not realize it, but after some conversation with the LLM we solved these problems. It actually led to some further learning process for us.

-   **What we had to correct or verify:**

    We simply had to do what the LLM told us to do and than had to check f it actually worked. If not we went back to the roots and started asking again why it did not work.

### Usage 4: Reviewing structure and wording

-   **Tool / model:**

    -   ChatGPT and Claude

-   **What it produced:**

    We used an LLM to review the structure and wording of parts of the proposal, README, analysis and reflection.

-   **What we had to correct or verify:**

    Not a lot, just that nothing changed and that the outcome meets our expectations.
